title: Juniper Networks Junos OS Evolved Pre-Authentication Root RCE via Exposed Anomaly Detection REST API on PTX Series Routers: watchTowr Research Confirms Single-Packet Full Device Takeover (CVE-2026-21902)
affected_products: Juniper Networks PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved 25.4R1-EVO through 25.4R1-S1-EVO (pre-patch)
Juniper Networks PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved 25.4 releases prior to 25.4R2-EVO

timeline.event: Juniper Networks SIRT issues out-of-band security advisory JSA107128 disclosing CVE-2026-21902 in Junos OS Evolved On-Box Anomaly Detection Framework; patches released simultaneously

watchTowr Labs publishes detailed technical research 'Sometimes You Can Just Feel the Security in the Design' with full root RCE exploit analysis and detection artifact generator; BleepingComputer and multiple security outlets report the vulnerability
Technical blog post 'One Packet to Root' published with exploitation mechanics; security community confirms single unauthenticated HTTP request achieves root code execution
Government agencies issue alerts: Singapore CSA (AL-2026-020) and Canadian CCCS (AV26-172) warn operators of PTX Series routers to patch immediately
UpGuard publishes organizational risk assessment; RunZero scans reveal exposed port 8160 instances across internet-facing PTX router deployments

No active exploitation in the wild confirmed as of March 15, 2026; patch availability and rapid disclosure may have prevented weaponization, but vulnerability remains critical and high-risk for unpatched systems

simple_summary.what_happened: Researchers at watchTowr discovered that Juniper Networks' PTX Series core routers (running Junos OS Evolved) contained a built-in diagnostic service called the On-Box Anomaly Detection Framework that was accidentally exposed to the internet without any authentication. This service runs with full root privileges, meaning any attacker who can reach port 8160 on an affected router can send a single network request and instantly take over the entire device. Juniper patched the flaw alongside its discovery, but any unpatched router remains fully exposed.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running Juniper Networks PTX Series core routers with Junos OS Evolved version 25.4R1-EVO through 25.4R1-S1-EVO. PTX Series routers are high-capacity core routing platforms used primarily by telecommunications carriers, internet service providers, large financial institutions, and enterprises with major network infrastructure. The vulnerability does NOT affect standard (non-Evolved) Junos OS.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker scans the internet for Juniper PTX routers with port 8160/TCP open - the anomaly detection REST API port. Step 2: Attacker sends a single unauthenticated HTTP request to the On-Box Anomaly Detection Framework REST API endpoint on port 8160. Step 3: Because the service binds to all interfaces (0.0.0.0) and has no authentication, the request is accepted with no credentials required. Step 4: The attacker's payload executes arbitrary code as the root user - the highest privilege level on the device. Step 5: Attacker now has complete control of the core router: can modify routing tables, capture all transit traffic, disable network segments, create persistent backdoors, and use the router as a launch point for attacks on all connected networks.
what_you_should_do: Upgrade affected PTX Series routers to Junos OS Evolved 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO, or 26.2R1-EVO immediately
If patching is not immediately possible, disable the vulnerable service using 'request pfe anomalies disable' command
Implement firewall filters and ACLs to block all external access to port 8160/TCP; restrict to trusted management networks only
Scan your environment for internet-facing PTX routers with port 8160 exposed using network scanning tools
Monitor for any unauthenticated HTTP requests to port 8160 on affected devices as an indicator of exploit attempts
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: Core routers are the backbone of large network infrastructures. Unlike a compromised endpoint or server, a compromised core router gives an attacker complete visibility into all traffic passing through it - potentially terabits of data per second in carrier environments. The fact that this requires zero authentication and only one network packet makes it exceptionally easy to exploit. Telecommunications providers and ISPs using PTX Series routers could have their entire transit infrastructure silently compromised. watchTowr's research confirms the vulnerability is trivially exploitable.

tactics.description: Unauthenticated attacker exploits the exposed On-Box Anomaly Detection REST API on port 8160/TCP to gain immediate root code execution on Juniper PTX Series routers
Arbitrary OS commands executed as root via the Python-based REST API service with no sandboxing or privilege separation
Attacker can install persistent backdoors in router configuration or startup scripts after gaining root access
Compromised core router can be used to capture and exfiltrate all transit traffic passing through the device at line rate
Root access on core router enables lateral movement to all directly connected network segments and peered networks

detection_guidance.summary: Detection centers on identifying port 8160 exposure and any HTTP requests to the Anomaly Detection Framework REST API. watchTowr has released an open-source Detection Artifact Generator (GitHub) that can validate whether a device is vulnerable and generate detection artifacts for security tools. Network scanning to identify exposed instances should be the first priority.

log_sources.description: Monitor for any inbound TCP connections to port 8160 from untrusted source IPs targeting Juniper PTX routers
Look for REST API access log entries on port 8160 from unexpected source addresses
Identify any flow records showing external connections to port 8160 on PTX router management IPs
Monitor for unexpected child processes spawned by the anomaly detection service, which may indicate code execution

what_to_look_for.indicator: Inbound TCP connections to port 8160 from untrusted external IPs
what_to_look_for.description: The On-Box Anomaly Detection REST API listens on port 8160 and should never receive connections from external untrusted sources; any such connection is suspicious
what_to_look_for.log_field: dst_port=8160 AND src_ip NOT IN trusted_mgmt_range

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP GET/POST requests to Anomaly Detection API endpoints from external addresses
what_to_look_for.description: Exploitation involves HTTP requests to the REST API; look for API calls to /anomaly or related endpoints from non-management source IPs

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected process execution from anomaly detection service parent process
what_to_look_for.description: Successful exploitation results in arbitrary OS commands running as root under the anomaly detection Python process
what_to_look_for.log_field: parent_process='anomaly_detection' AND child_process NOT IN expected_processes

what_to_look_for.indicator: Exposed port 8160 on internet-facing PTX router interfaces
what_to_look_for.description: Scan all PTX router IP ranges to identify any device with port 8160 accessible from untrusted networks using Shodan, Censys, or internal scanners

immediate_actions.action: Upgrade to patched Junos OS Evolved: 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO, or 26.2R1-EVO
immediate_actions.description: Juniper has released patches concurrently with disclosure. Upgrade immediately. Verify the current version on all PTX Series routers and prioritize those with port 8160 internet-accessible.

immediate_actions.action: Disable the On-Box Anomaly Detection service if patching is delayed
immediate_actions.description: Until the patch is applied, disable the vulnerable service using the Junos OS CLI command. This prevents exploitation but may affect built-in diagnostics.

immediate_actions.action: Block TCP port 8160 using firewall filters and ACLs at network perimeter
immediate_actions.description: Apply firewall filters to block external access to port 8160 on all PTX router interfaces. Only allow connections from explicitly trusted management network ranges.
immediate_actions.command: set firewall family inet filter BLOCK-8160 term deny-external from destination-port 8160

immediate_actions.action: Run watchTowr Detection Artifact Generator to verify exposure and generate detection content
immediate_actions.description: Use the open-source tool released by watchTowr Labs to validate vulnerability status on your devices and generate detection artifacts for your security tools.
